title: Plant one. Guard the ones you have.
norm: Plant a tree where it can grow - and look after the grown trees already shading your street instead of letting them go.
wit: The best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago. The second best is this monsoon.
goesRight: Streets stay shaded and walkable through the hottest months, homes need less cooling, and the neighbourhood is greener and calmer.

impact.cause: A tree's canopy shading the pavement, walls, and people below
impact.mechanism: Leaves block incoming sunlight before it heats surfaces, and water evaporating from them carries heat away
impact.effect: Cooler pavements, homes, and walking routes in hot weather
impact.source_title: The Cooling Potential of Urban Trees
impact.supported_by: trees can improve thermal comfort by 2-8 degrees C
impact.caveat: A global WRI synthesis, not an India measurement; real cooling varies with canopy, species, and climate, so the number is kept out of the claim.

impact.cause: Denser, healthier tree canopy across an Indian city
impact.mechanism: Canopy shade cuts the sunlight reaching the ground and lowers land-surface temperature
impact.effect: Generally cooler conditions, though how much depends on the local climate
impact.source_title: Cooling cities needs climate-smart design, not just more trees
impact.supported_by: Urban trees generally help cool cities by providing shade, reducing incoming solar radiation, and lowering surface temperatures, these benefits vary in different cities.
impact.caveat: The study reported here found that in hot, compact, humid cities very dense canopy can add moisture and raise the felt heat, so cooling is real but climate-dependent.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: A grown tree is decades of shade.
norm: Before cutting down a healthy old tree or paving over its roots, look hard for another way - its shade and cooling take decades to replace.
wit: You can plant a sapling today. You cannot fast-forward it.
goesRight: A grown canopy keeps cooling the street and filtering the air for years, and the ground stays soft instead of turning into a hot, hard slab.

impact.cause: A large mature tree left standing, still shading and cooling around it
impact.mechanism: A full canopy shades a wide area and cools the air as water evaporates from its leaves - an effect that can't be recovered fast, because it depends on a size that takes decades to grow
impact.effect: Losing the tree removes real, passive cooling from that spot
impact.source_title: Tackling Extreme Heat through Urban Forestry in Delhi
impact.supported_by: Trees are particularly powerful and can cool their surroundings with the equivalent power of a 7 kilowatt air conditioner by providing shade and cooling the air through evapotranspiration
impact.caveat: An illustrative comparison RMI cites from external work, not a measured value for one Indian tree; kept out of the claim.

impact.cause: Replacing a tree and its soil with concrete or paving
impact.mechanism: Hard, dark surfaces soak up and store heat, and the shade and evaporation the tree gave are gone
impact.effect: The spot becomes hotter than a comparable green or open surface
impact.source_title: Tackling Extreme Heat through Urban Forestry in Delhi
impact.supported_by: Cities are often 2°C to 9°C (3.6°F–16.2°F) hotter than surrounding peri-urban and rural areas
impact.caveat: RMI's general urban-heat-island range across cities, not the measured effect of removing one tree; kept out of the claim.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Green cover cools - and cleans - the air.
norm: Protect the parks, avenue trees, and green patches near you, and back local greening - shade cools the street and leaves catch some of the dust we breathe.
wit: A park is the cheapest air-conditioner a city ever bought.
goesRight: Streets with canopy and parks feel cooler in summer and hold a little less dust in the air, easing the load on the people most exposed to it.

impact.cause: More tree canopy and green cover across a neighbourhood
impact.mechanism: Shade and evaporation lower surface temperatures and how hot it actually feels to stand outside
impact.effect: Less heat stress, and more bearable conditions in green areas than in bare, built-up ones
impact.source_title: Tackling Extreme Heat through Urban Forestry in Delhi
impact.supported_by: Green cover reduces 'felt' (physiologically equivalent) temperatures by 5–14 percent
impact.caveat: A figure RMI cites from external nature-based-solutions research; it varies by site, so it is kept out of the claim.

impact.cause: Fine particles (PM2.5) passing over and settling on leaves
impact.mechanism: Particles deposit on and cling to leaf and stem surfaces, and leaves take in some gases - briefly removing them from the air people breathe
impact.effect: A modest local dip in particles near tree cover - a buffer, never a substitute for cutting pollution at its source
impact.source_title: Air Pollution Removal by Urban Forests
impact.supported_by: Fine particulate matter is deposited on tree surfaces clinging to leaves and stems.
impact.caveat: Modelled for U.S. parks; capture is partial and temporary - particles can wash off - so trees complement, not replace, cutting pollution at source.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.
